title: practical english usage - Part 42
content:
A  progressive form can be used to talk about a continuous activity.

I'll have spent all my savings by the end of the year.

The builders say they will have finished the roof by Tuesday.

by a certain time in the future.

We   can use the future perfect to say that something will be finished or complete will have + past participle 40 future     perfect     ________________ In normal usage, we  prefer will, must or should to express ideas of this kind.

The hirer shall be responsible for maintenance of the vehicle.

subjects to refer to obligations and duties, in both British and American English.

In contracts and other legal documents, shall is often used with third-person 8  obligation:       shall For more about this and other uses of will, ►    69.5, 73, 79-80.

It's no use expecting Barry to turn up. He'll have forgotten.

As you will have noticed, there is a new secretary in the front office.

Will have ... can express similar ideas about the past.

Don't phone them now - they'll be having dinner.

'There's somebody at the door.' 'That'll be the electrician.' or certainly the case.

We   can use will to talk about the present - to say what we  think is very probably 7 expressing        certainty       about     the   present      or  past Will you be doing the shopping...?

Are you doing the shopping this afternoon?

When are you going to see Andy?

or the future progressive (►  41).

To  ask for information about planned actions, we  use a present form (►  35-36) Will you do the shopping this afternoon, please?

Will you turn off that music!

request (►  80.1).

With a verb referring to an action, will you ...? usually introduces an order or Will you be here next week?

How soon will you know your travel dates?

With a verb referring to a state, will you ...? asks for information.

6 different       meanings       of  will    you     .. .?

grammar •  41   future progressive lunch), ►    39.7.

For will be.. .ing used to express certainty about the present (e.g. Don't phone now - they'll be having I'm going to be working all day tomorrow, so I won't have time to shop.

A  progressive form of the going to structure is also possible.

3 progressive         form    with    going       to Will you be wanting lunch tomorrow?

forms (►  4).

This usage is possible with verbs that do not normally have progressive Are you going to stay in this evening? (pressing for a decision) want to know your plans') Will you be staying in this evening? (very polite enquiry, suggesting 'I simply Compare: shows that he/she does not want to influence the listener's intentions.) the future progressive to ask 'What have you already decided?', the speaker The  tense can be used to make polite enquiries about people's plans. (By using 'It's OK. I'll be going to the shops anyway.' 'Shall I pick up the laundry for you? 'Oh, no, don't make a special journey.' but about things that will happen 'anyway'.

This is useful if we  want to show that we  are not talking about making decisions, I'll be seeing you one of these days, I expect.

same time next week.

Professor Baxter will be giving another lecture on Roman glass-making at the decided, or which  are expected to happen in the normal course of events.

The  future progressive is often used to refer to future events which  are fixed or 2 events     that    are   fixed    or  expected       to  happen Good luck with the exam. We'll be thinking of you.

This time tomorrow I'll be lying on the beach.

(happening) at a particular moment in the future.

We   can use the future progressive to say that something will be in progress 1 events     in  progress       in  the   future shall/will + be + .. .ing 41 future     progressive Section 4 Talking     about     the   Future grammar •  42  be to + infinitive: I am to ..., you are to ..., etc I looked out of the window, but there was nothing to be seen.

There's nothing to be done.   She was nowhere to be found.

Some other common expressions with be + passive infinitive: To be taken three times a day after meals. (on a medicine bottle) Sometimes only the passive infinitive is used.
